segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Chapter Seventeen: The Father's Hands

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The workshop lights were on.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stopped at the corner of Ash Street and looked at the orange warmth behind the glass, the shadow moving inside. Not sitting. Moving with purpose, the steady back-and-forth of the lathe running, and he could hear it from the street — the low mechanical whir underneath the evening's ambient murmur, the sound the city made when it was winding down and the careful speech of the inner districts relaxed into something almost like noise.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Three minutes he'd been standing there. He knew because he'd counted.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The plan had people in it now. It had Dav Sorn and Vella Ashvale and Torvald Hess and Mira Fen, who had burned one page and written it again. It had a shape. It had a date. What it didn't have was the Bellwright family's formal objection, filed with the Academy, signed and sung by the head of the household — and the head of the household was in there turning a clapper on the lathe, and Cass had been standing in the cold long enough that his ears hurt.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He went in.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The smell hit him first — linseed oil, metal-dust, the faint char of the forge even though the forge had been cold for hours. The workshop held warmth the way a bell held sound, radiating it back slowly long after the source was gone. His father stood at the lathe with his back to the door, both hands on the tool rest. The clapper turning between the centers caught the lamplight in a slow bright revolution. His father's hands were not shaking.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass watched him work. One full revolution. Two. The shavings of bronze curled away in thin bright ribbons and fell. He'd been watching his father's hands his whole life — at the dinner table, passing the bread, reaching for the salt, signing documents at the Academy in that particular careful way with one hand steadying the other. He had watched them shake in the kitchen over the oil jar two weeks ago, when his father had finally looked at the journals across the table and said

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] *you should read them.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had watched them shake on the walk to the Academy, on the walk home, at every meal since.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: In the workshop they were still. They were always still in the workshop. Cass had known this since he was small and had never said it aloud and did not say it now.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I need your help,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The lathe slowed. His father reached for the lever and brought it to a stop, and the whir dropped through its registers and went silent, and the workshop settled into a quiet that had the quality of aftermath — the air still vibrating faintly where the sound had been.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Eddan turned around. He held the cutting tool loosely in one hand, not set down yet.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Tell me,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass told him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stood in the workshop and talked for ten minutes without stopping. The longest he'd spoken aloud in weeks. He told his father about Dav Sorn and the twenty years of Founding Day silence, the thing Dav had felt in the square without understanding what it was. He told him about Vella Ashvale and the vote she regretted and how Lenne had been sitting two chairs away and hadn't looked at her mother. He told him about Mira Fen's twelve pages, and about Torvald Hess and thirty years of tally-songs and hands stained blue-green that no amount of washing shifted. He told him about the Civic Pitch assessment — the formal hearing, the Academy venue, the live evaluation that the co-custodial transfer required under the Codex's own rules.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He told him what he was going to do in that room. What he was going to sing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father listened. He set the cutting tool down on the workbench at some point, and after that his hands were still, and Cass kept talking because his father was not interrupting.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father said:

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: You want me to contest the transfer in front of the Academy.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: And at the live assessment, you're going to sing the question into audible range.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Eddan looked at him steadily.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: You know what happens if the city hears it and says no.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The pause was long enough that Cass counted it. Four seconds. Five. His father's hands rested on the workbench, both of them, flat and still.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: And if they say yes?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Then for the first time,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said,

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: it means something.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Something moved across his father's face — not quite readable, somewhere between the expression he wore when a bell came out of the mold wrong and the expression he wore when it came out exactly right.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: And if they say nothing?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass had thought about this. On the walk over, in the cold, while he was standing at the corner of Ash Street looking at the lit windows.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Then we'll know what silence sounds like when it's chosen.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father turned away. He picked up the cutting tool and set it down again in a slightly different place, which accomplished nothing, and then he turned back.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: All right,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I'll file the objection tomorrow.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass's ribs loosened. He hadn't known they were tight.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: [tense] There's something I need to tell you first,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: his father said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His voice was different. Leveled, the way you leveled a surface before you put something heavy on it. Cass went still.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The three bells,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Eddan said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: In the locked room.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The workshop bells on their frames lifted faintly — a sympathetic vibration from some frequency Cass wasn't consciously producing. He caught himself and stopped.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I know you've been in there,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: his father said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I've known for a while. I should have told you sooner.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused, and the pause was not evasion but weight, the weight of a man choosing his words as you chose a tool for a specific bore.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The three bells are not tuned to the question. The frequency you've been hearing. They're tuned to amplify it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass waited.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: If someone sang the right fragment in that room — the right pitch, the right sequence — the bells would carry it. Not just echo it. Bring it up from below hearing into audible range.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father looked at the workbench, at the bronze shavings curled on the stone floor.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The room is built for it. The dimensions. The wall treatment. I thought it was a memorial, when I was young. Something Aldric built to preserve the sound. But that's not what it is.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He built it to use,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: He built it to use.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father's voice was flat.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The journals say he always intended for someone to sing the question into audible range. The amplification is built into the system. Into the bells themselves. He tuned it in when he cast them. He locked the room and gave the key to his son and said: when someone is ready.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: He didn't say ready for what. He didn't say when.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He was hoping,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: [softly] I think he didn't know how. I think he built the instrument and ran out of courage for the rest. And he thought — maybe his son would. Or his grandson. Or someone.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He picked up a bronze shaving from the workbench, turned it between his fingers.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The night I read the journals. After the signing. I understood what the room was for. I understood what I'd traded, and what I could have done instead. Both at once.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set the bronze shaving down.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: [slowly] The hands started the next morning.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass heard it. The sentence arrived and stayed there, and he heard it the way you heard a note struck in a quiet room — clean, with nothing around it to absorb it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] *The hands started the next morning.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had been watching his father's hands shake his entire life. At the dinner table, passing the bread. Reaching for the salt. He had been watching it since he was small enough that he'd thought it was just the way his father's hands worked, the same way some people had crooked teeth or walked with a lean. He had never asked. He had never asked because asking would make it a thing they talked about, and he was afraid of the answer.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Now he had it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The night of the signing, his father had read the journals. He had read what Aldric built into the bells, and he had read what he himself had just traded away, and the next morning his hands had begun to shake. Not from age. From a weight that had nowhere else to go. Ten years. Cass had been four years old.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: How old was he?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: When you signed.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Sixteen.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He was sixteen.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His voice came out wrong — too flat, the flatness of something that was going to be loud if he let it. He didn't let it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: He was sixteen and you signed.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: And you knew.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass's hands were at his sides, and his left hand pressed flat against his thigh, pressing down.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You knew what was in the journals. You'd read them. You knew there was another way.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I read them after,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: his father said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: That night. You read them that same night.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: So you knew by morning.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass's voice had gotten quieter, which was worse than if it had gotten louder.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [angry] You knew by morning that you'd traded him for the bells and that there was a way you could have fought it instead, and you just — you went back to work.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father did not answer. Which was its own kind of answer.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: For ten years,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Ten years you knew. I've been in pain every day of my life because I can hear what's wrong with this city, and you knew what it was and you said nothing, and Perin has been composing contracts for Maret Corda since he was sixteen years old because you chose the bells over your own —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The workshop was very quiet. The lamplight was the same. The bells on their frames were still. Cass's left hand was pressed so hard against his thigh that he'd feel it tomorrow.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father had not moved. He stood with his hands flat on the workbench, not looking away, and his face had the look of a man who had decided he was going to hear whatever came, because he had not heard it for ten years and that was the debt he owed.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at him. He looked at his father's hands, steady on the workbench, the bronze shaving sitting between two fingers where he'd set it down.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [slowly] The hands started the next morning,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said again. Not angry this time. Just saying it back. Making sure it was real.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Does Perin know? That you know?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: No.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Does he know you've been sorry?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Another pause. Longer.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: [quietly] I don't know what he knows.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked at the oil jar on the bench. Small, clay, cork-stopped, completely ordinary, the kind of object that had been in the workshop his whole life and had never meant anything until it had meant everything. He looked at it for a long time.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You should write him,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said finally.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You should tell him yourself. Not me.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I know.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm not —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass stopped. Started again.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm not saying it's fixed. I'm not saying it's fine. I don't know what I'm saying.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: You don't have to say anything.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm fourteen,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said. Not what he meant but was also exactly what he meant — the full weight of it, of being fourteen and having just been handed ten years of his family's history in a single sentence, of having just been angry at his father in a way he'd never been angry before, of not knowing what came after that.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'm fourteen and I don't know what I'm supposed to do with this.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Nothing,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: his father said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Tonight, nothing. Tomorrow, we work.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: That was probably the most his father had ever said about feelings in Cass's entire life.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass breathed in. Breathed out. The pressure behind his eye was not the needle-heat of an under-note — just the ordinary pressure of a person who had been angry and was now tired.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Okay,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: There's something else.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father's voice had shifted again — still leveled, but with something underneath it that Cass recognized as the sound of a man who had decided to say the whole thing, not just the part that was easier.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: The fragment. The one in Perin's letters. You've assembled it?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass looked up.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You knew about the letters.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I suspected. I didn't want to know for certain.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father met his eyes.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Do you have the full sequence?

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father nodded.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Then go to the room. The key's on the board.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The key was on the board where it had always been, hanging on a small iron hook between the pitch-gauge and the spare clapper mounts. Cass had taken it once before in secret, at night, with his heart going at the wrong tempo. He took it now while his father watched, in the lamplight, openly, and neither of them said anything about the difference.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The door off the kitchen was the same door it had always been. Dark wood, old iron latch, the smell of a room that didn't get much air. Cass put the key in and turned it and pushed the door open.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The three bells were exactly as he'd left them. Small, bronze-dark, hanging from the low ceiling on their iron mounts. The air in the room was still and cold — colder than the kitchen, the kind of cold that accumulated in enclosed spaces and didn't leave.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stood in the doorway and looked at the walls. He could see the treatment now that he knew to look for it — a faint variation in the stone's surface, long horizontal passes, someone working by hand, building something that would last two hundred years. Not decoration. Function.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stepped in and closed the door.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The silence changed immediately.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: That was the first thing — not a dramatic thing, just a fact, the way the acoustics shifted when he pulled the door shut. The ambient noise of the house, the building-settle sounds, the distant murmur of the city, the tiny percussive sounds of a house at night — all of it cut off. What remained was the silence that was always there underneath those sounds, the silence that was usually inaudible because there was always something covering it. Cass's ears adjusted. He could hear his own breathing. His heartbeat. The small sounds his clothes made when he shifted his weight.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stood there for a moment in that clean, dense silence and thought about Perin. Not the abstract Perin of the locked room and the signed contract — the specific Perin who had sent seventeen letters over four years with two or three intervals embedded in each one, hidden in technical observations, building a phrase across years the way you built a bell, slowly, in pieces, each piece meaningless alone.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] *The resonance chamber on the east wing runs a minor third sharp of the reference pitch.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] *The clapper stock here is cast too light — it rings a quarter-tone above center.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had assembled the sequence three months ago, late, with the letters spread across his desk and his hands shaking faintly, and he had hummed the first four bars and stopped immediately because even that, even at near-silence, had felt like standing too close to something very large.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He opened his mouth and sang.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Quietly at first — the pitch sitting below the Civic Pitch's lowest registered note, below the bass string of any instrument the Academy owned, in the quarter-tone gap between F and F-sharp where no scale the Academy recognized had a home. The frequency with no name. He had been approximating it for weeks. Now, in this room, his ear found it precisely the way a tongue found a split lip — not searching, just knowing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The bells moved before he'd finished the first phrase.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He felt it in his chest first. A warmth that started behind his sternum and spread outward along his ribs, the way heat spread through a struck bell — not the sound yet, but the bell's whole body beginning to participate. Then the room changed. Not the sound getting louder; something else, something he didn't have a word for. The air thickened the way air thickened before a storm, a pressure change you felt in your ears before you understood it. The floor was the same floor it had always been, cold stone under his feet, but it felt different — as though it had weight now that it hadn't had before, as though the stone itself had become aware of what was happening in the room above it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He kept singing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The bells were amplifying it. That was what his father had said — not just echoing, not just the passive sympathetic resonance he'd felt a dozen times in the workshop when his father struck a test tone and the other bells hummed in answer. This was active, directional, the way a resonance chamber shaped a voice but more than that, more focused, the frequency rising in the small room with a speed that pressed against his ears and the bones behind them and the roof of his mouth and the cartilage of his ribs where they joined the breastbone. His chest was resonating with it. The boundary between his body and the room went uncertain — he was breathing, he was singing, and the room was singing back at a volume that was not loud but was everywhere at once.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: And then something opened.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had been hearing this frequency his entire life. In courtrooms, in the market, in his own kitchen when someone said something they hadn't chosen to say. He had heard it as a thread, a needle, a thin and painful thing that slid behind his left eye and stayed there, hot and precise. The under-note. The frequency that shouldn't exist in any scale the city recognized.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: This was not that.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: This was what that frequency actually was, at its full size. He had been hearing a signal through static for fourteen years — a fragment of music coming through a wall, muffled, distorted by everything the city had built around it to keep it small. The bells had just turned up the volume, and for the first time he could hear that there had always been music playing under the static. Not a needle. Not a thread. Something with depth to it, with dimension, a note that had a floor and a ceiling and sides. It occupied the room the way a person occupied a room. It had been here all along, compressed below hearing, and now it was simply, completely present.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The pain was gone.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: That was the thing that stopped his breath mid-phrase and nearly broke the sequence — the absence of the pain he'd always associated with this frequency. The under-note had always hurt because it was constrained, pushed below the range where sound was supposed to live, pressed into a space too small for it. Here, given room, it opened. The pressure behind his eye was gone. What he felt instead was a vibration in his chest that he recognized from one other moment in his life: Founding Day, when all forty-seven bells rang in sequence and the under-note went completely silent. That same quality. A thing being the size it was meant to be.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He found the pitch again and finished the sequence.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Eight bars. Perin's sequence, the one assembled from seventeen letters across four years, each interval hidden in a technical observation, building the phrase piece by piece the way you built a bell — the first bar in a letter about the Corda resonance chambers, the second in a letter about clapper stock, the third in a letter about weather. Cass had the whole thing in his body now, memorized past thought into muscle and breath and the specific tension of his jaw. He sang it fully, and the bells carried it, and the room held it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: For eight bars, the question that had been sitting below audible range for two hundred years was simply present.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped singing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The bells rang on. Not fading immediately — building slightly, the room taking a breath and finishing the phrase on its own, the frequency filling every corner of the small stone space, and then peaking, a long sustained moment, and then beginning to drop. Slowly, by degrees, through the registers, bass and sub-bass and the frequency below frequency, until it was gone.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The silence afterward was the cleanest thing he'd ever heard.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stood with his hands at his sides. His ribs were still faintly vibrating, the body's echo of a sound that was already gone. His jaw ached along its hinges from sustaining a pitch he'd been approximating for weeks and had just, for the first time, found precisely. He was aware of his own heartbeat.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] He thought of Perin composing that sequence. Perin at twenty, twenty-two, twenty-four, sitting in the Corda compound with its well-tuned resonance chambers and its retainer Court Singers, writing letters home about the weather and the quality of the bronze stock and the east wing's acoustic problems, and hiding inside those letters, in the intervals, in the technical observations that only Cass would understand, a question. A message. *I'm still here. I know what this is. I'm still trying.* Four years of letters. Thirty-four intervals. Eight bars.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] Cass had been fourteen years old and in constant pain and he had heard every one.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He opened the door and went back to the workshop.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father was at the bench, standing with his hands flat on the wood. He looked up when Cass came in.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: It worked,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father nodded once. His hands were still.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Did you know it would?

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I hoped.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father looked at him steadily.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I've been hoping for ten years.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: [softly] I'm sorry it took me this long to tell you.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: This was as close to an apology as Eddan Bellwright had probably ever come in his life. Cass heard it for what it was. He didn't say *it's fine* because it wasn't, and his father wasn't asking him to pretend it was.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I'll need to practice the pitch,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The exact frequency. I've been approximating it from the letters but I need to know it precisely.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Come back tomorrow morning. Before the Civic Pitch. We'll find it.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Okay.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Neither of them moved for a moment. The workshop bells hummed faintly on their frames. His father's hands were on the bench, flat and still.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I'll file the —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Eddan stopped.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: First thing. The Academy opens at seven.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: I'll be there.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Dav Sorn is filing the petition. Vella Ashvale has already ratified it.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Something moved in his father's face — surprise, or the specific expression of a man recalculating what he thought he knew.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Vella Ashvale.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: And Torvald Hess. And Mira Fen.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father was quiet for a moment.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: You've been busy.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: EDDAN
segments.text: Good,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: his father said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He wrote the letter that night at the kitchen table, by lamplight, while his father's footsteps moved back and forth in the workshop through the wall. He had a sheet of Academy correspondence paper and a pen that needed re-dipping every other line.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He started three times.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The first attempt: *Perin, I found the room and I understand now what the letters were for, and there are people who are going to help and Dav Sorn has filed a petition and Vella Ashvale has ratified it and—* He got six lines and stopped. Maret Corda's people could read this. Anything they read should not contain the word *amplification* or the word *petition* or anything that let her know what was coming before it came.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The second attempt: *Perin, I need you to know—* He stopped at that. He didn't know how to finish it. He needed Perin to know too many things, and most of them couldn't go in a letter.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at the blank paper for a moment. The lamp flame moved in a draft from under the kitchen door. His father's footsteps through the wall, steady, back and forth, the sound of a man doing the one thing that settled his hands.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He started again.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] *Perin,* he said aloud, quietly, as he wrote the name. He did that sometimes when he was writing something that mattered — said the words aloud as he wrote them, as if the saying made them more real than the ink did.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] *I found the room.* He said it as he wrote it. *I found the instrument. I know what the letters were for.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped and looked at that for a moment.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] *I'm going to use it.* He said that too, quietly, to the kitchen, to the lamp, to the wall between him and his father's workshop. *I don't know what will happen. But I want you to know that I heard you.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He wrote it down. Then: *Every interval. Every letter. I heard you.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at that line for a moment. Then he signed it — not *your brother* or *Cass from home* or any of the other ways he'd drafted it in his head. Just *Cass.* Simple, as you signed a thing you were willing to be held to.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He folded the paper and sealed it with the kitchen's wax. He wrote Perin's name on the outside. *Perin Bellwright, in care of the House of Corda. The Merchant Tier, south compound.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He left it on the kitchen table and went to bed.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He lay in the dark for a long time. His ribs were still faintly warm from the room, from the frequency that had been in there waiting. He thought about Aldric Bellwright building that room, tuning those three bells, locking the door. Giving the key to his son and saying *when someone is ready* and then dying, and his son dying, and his son's son, and all the years of Civic Pitches and ratified contracts and Volume Four's blank pages, and the question sitting below hearing, waiting.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A hundred and fifty years of waiting.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass wasn't sure he was ready. He was fairly sure it didn't matter.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The lathe started again through the wall — the low whir, the sound of his father's hands finding the only peace they had. Tomorrow: the objection filed, the petition ratified, the plan in motion. Tomorrow morning, before the Civic Pitch, his father would help him find the exact frequency, and he would learn the pitch precisely instead of by approximation, and the sequence that Perin had hidden across seventeen letters would become the thing it had always been trying to be.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The letter sat on the kitchen table, sealed, waiting for morning.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] *Every interval. Every letter. I heard you.*

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Outside, the city made its night sounds. A cart on cobblestones, a door closing, the ambient murmur of ten thousand people doing the careful, considered work of living in a place where words had weight. The Bell Tower was dark at this hour. The forty-seven bells hung in their mounts, silent, tuned to frequencies that had been waiting two hundred years for someone to decide to ask.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass closed his eyes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He slept.
